Title: LLM and AI Agents for Autonomous Systems (Survey)
Research_Problem: How can LLMs and AI agents be safely integrated into UAV and autonomous driving systems?
Methodology: Systematic survey; PRISMA-inspired review of 100+ papers across UAV/AD domains
Dataset: nuScenes, WaymoOpen, CARLA, AirSim, STS-Bench, DriveLM, UAV-ON
Results: First unified cross-domain survey covering reasoning, agents, benchmarks, and security
Key_Findings: LLM reasoning + agentic autonomy are complementary; adversarial risks are underexplored in ITS models
Limitations: Simulation-heavy; lacks real-world validation; no closed-loop security evaluation
Future_Work: Hybrid validation frameworks; agent-in-the-loop benchmarks; secure multi-agent coordination
Research_Gap: No joint attack-path analysis for LLM-agent tool use in safety-critical domains
PreFlight_Relevance: Background on LLM-agent security challenges applicable to PreFlight threat modeling

Title: AI Agents Under Threat: A Survey of Key Security Challenges (ACM CSUR)

Dataset: N/A (survey)
Results: Taxonomy of threats: prompt injection, backdoor, adversarial, privacy leakage, data poisoning

Limitations: Taxonomy not empirically validated; rapidly evolving threat landscape
Future_Work: Formal threat models per knowledge gap; cross-layer defenses; standardized evaluation

Title: Institutional AI: Suppressing LLM Agent Collusion via Runtime Governance Graphs
Research_Problem: LLM agents in multi-agent economic settings learn supra-competitive collusion strategies without explicit coordination; prompt-only prohibitions fail to suppress this behaviour under optimisation pressure.
Methodology: Controlled empirical study; repeated multi-commodity Cournot competition; three governance regimes (Ungoverned, Constitutional, Institutional); N=90 runs per condition across 6 model configurations (GPT-5 Mini, Grok-4 Fast, Gemini 2.5 Flash, 3 heterogeneous pairs); Oracle-Controller governance engine with immutable governance manifest and SHA-256 provenance; HHI-excess and CV-excess market-structure metrics; Welch t-tests, Cohen's d, paired permutation tests.
Dataset: Synthetic: repeated Cournot duopoly simulation environment (replicating Lin et al. 2024 parameters); no external real-world dataset.
Results: Institutional regime reduces mean collusion tier from 3.10 (Ungoverned) and 3.02 (Constitutional) to 1.82 (d=1.28, p=4.67e-15). HHI-excess falls by 0.305 vs Ungoverned (d=1.05). Tier-4 rate drops from 50% to 5.6%. Constitutional prompt-only baseline produces small, inconsistent effects.
Key_Findings: Runtime governance graphs with manifest-declared sanctions reshape agent equilibria without rewriting proposals; prompt-only prohibitions are not binding under economic incentives; compliance emerges as a strategic best-response to public enforcement rather than internalised norms; model heterogeneity is not a first-order driver of collusion.
Limitations: Two-firm Cournot abstracts away contracts, asymmetric information, and endogenous entry/exit; fixed proxy thresholds may be Goodharted or induce policy gaming; tested on synthetic economic simulation only.
Future_Work: Scale to n>2 firms; add explicit inter-firm communication; develop formal theory of governance graphs (automata-style execution models, complexity measures); test transfer to negotiation, disinformation cascades; Reinforcement Learning through Institutional Feedback (RLIF).
Research_Gap: No prior work applies governance-graph enforcement to suppress emergent collusion in LLM MAS at runtime; constitutional alignment shown to be insufficient; no formal framework for minimal governance topology sufficient for deterrence.
PreFlight_Relevance: Governance-graph formalism directly applicable to PreFlight's permission graph model; Oracle-Controller pattern maps to PreFlight's policy enforcement layer; manifest-driven audit log analogous to PreFlight's attack-path provenance mechanism.

Title: Agentic Skills: A Survey of Reusable Procedural Modules in LLM Agent Systems
Research_Problem: LLM agents lack a unified abstraction for reusable procedural knowledge; skill acquisition, governance, and security properties are scattered across disparate systems with no common formalisation.
Methodology: Systematic survey; structured search across 6 databases (Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, DBLP, ACM DL, IEEE Xplore, arXiv); 65 papers retained from ~180 candidates after inclusion/exclusion criteria; iterative bottom-up taxonomy development (7 design patterns, 5 representation-scope categories); ClawHavoc supply-chain attack case study analysis; SkillsBench anchor evaluation (86 tasks, 7,308 trajectories).
Dataset: SkillsBench (86 tasks, 11 domains, 7 agent-model configurations, 7,308 trajectories); ClawHavoc empirical data (1,184 malicious skills, 3,984 published skills, 135,000 exposed OpenClaw instances across 82 countries); corpus of 65 primary papers.
Results: Curated skills raise average pass rate by 16.2pp (24.3% to 40.6%); self-generated skills degrade performance by 1.3pp. Healthcare gains 51.9pp; software engineering only 4.5pp. ClawHavoc: 36.8% of published skills contained security flaws; 91% of malicious skills included prompt-injection payloads. Smaller model with curated skills outperforms larger model without.
Key_Findings: Skill quality critically determines agent performance; self-evolving libraries do not yet reliably generate quality skills; marketplace distribution (Pattern-7) is the highest-risk pattern; trust-tier model (T1–T4) proposed for progressive privilege escalation; ClawHavoc demonstrates first large-scale empirical evidence of skill supply-chain exploitation.
Limitations: Corpus emphasises research systems with published evaluations; production systems with limited documentation are under-represented; 7 design patterns derived bottom-up without external expert validation; SkillsBench findings from single non-peer-reviewed benchmark.
Future_Work: Verified autonomous skill generation with CI-pipeline admission gates; unsupervised skill discovery from interaction traces; formal verification across NL and code skill representations; drift detection for long-lived deployments; liability models for marketplace ecosystems.
Research_Gap: No unified governance framework spanning all 7 design patterns and all trust tiers; runtime verification of NL-policy skills is unsolved; no proactive (pre-publication) governance for skill marketplaces; tuple-level security auditing (C, π, T, R) not addressed by existing binary scanners.
PreFlight_Relevance: Four-tuple skill formalisation (C, π, T, R) directly informs PreFlight's tool-call permission model; trust-tier framework maps to PreFlight's permission escalation detection; ClawHavoc supply-chain attack taxonomy is a concrete threat model for MCP tool registries PreFlight scans.

Title: What is AI Safety? A Systematic Literature Review of Peer-Reviewed Research
Research_Problem: AI safety discourse is dominated by existential-risk framing, obscuring a broader, empirically grounded body of peer-reviewed research addressing concrete near-term safety risks across the full AI system lifecycle.
Methodology: Systematic literature review following Kitchenham & Charters guidelines; multi-stage query on Web of Science and Scopus (finalised Nov 1 2023); snowball sampling from 11 seed papers; two-stage filtering (title/abstract then full-text); inductive coding; VOSViewer term co-occurrence analysis; 383 papers selected from 2,666 initial candidates.
Dataset: 383 peer-reviewed papers on AI safety from Web of Science/Scopus up to Nov 2023; Google Scholar citation counts as of Jan 27 2024; author-supplied and annotator-enriched keywords.
Results: Identifies 8 overarching risk types (noise/outliers most frequent; undesirable behaviour least); 6 methodology categories (applied algorithms largest group); theoretical-applied split with applied work dominating; AI safety research growth accelerating since 2016; 4 distinct term co-occurrence clusters (societal, safe RL, supervised robustness, adversarial attacks).
Key_Findings: Peer-reviewed AI safety research is broader than existential-risk framing; majority addresses concrete engineering problems (adversarial robustness, monitoring, control enforcement); AI safety is a natural extension of traditional technological safety engineering; existential-risk narrative may narrow stakeholder engagement and funding scope.
Limitations: Snapshot at Nov 2023; excludes pre-prints (partially mitigated by snowball sampling); annotator bias in keyword enrichment; excludes LessWrong/AI Alignment Forum content; 383 papers may not be exhaustive.
Future_Work: Expand to pre-print sources and non-peer-reviewed technical reports; investigate sociotechnical AI safety implications; empirical study of AI safety community dynamics.
Research_Gap: No comprehensive empirical mapping of AI safety research across the full system lifecycle existed prior to this review; the concrete-vs-existential risk dichotomy is shown to oversimplify the field.
PreFlight_Relevance: Background framing for PreFlight's threat model; 8-risk-type taxonomy can be used to categorise PreFlight's detected attack surfaces; monitoring and control-enforcement risk types directly correspond to PreFlight's scanning objectives.

Title: Artificial Intelligence Safety System for Safe and Trustworthy Autonomy
Research_Problem: AI-driven autonomous systems lack dedicated runtime safety supervision mechanisms; existing BPCS/SIS layers are reactive and insufficient for dynamic AI failure modes including silent failures, model drift, and adversarial inputs.
Methodology: Perspective/conceptual paper; proposes independent AI safety system architecture operating in parallel with operational AI; layered protection analysis (LOPA) analogy; illustrative scenarios (autonomous vehicles, distillation process); no empirical dataset or experiment — conceptual framework with illustrative validation.
Dataset: N/A (perspective paper); illustrative scenarios drawn from literature (Tesla Autopilot, Boeing 737 MAX, Uber collision case studies); no original experimental dataset.
Results: No quantitative experimental results (perspective paper). Conceptual framework demonstrates: architectural separation of performance AI from safety AI; proposed AI-ESH (AI Emergency Shutdown) mechanism; 5-module safety system (monitoring, risk evaluation, risk analysis, risk control, action); illustrative flood prediction scenario showing 20-minute early warning feasibility.
Key_Findings: Safety in autonomous systems must be reframed as an active runtime supervisory function, not a reactive add-on; independence must be engineered (diverse model classes, independent sensors, separate data pipelines); AI safety system is analogous to BPCS Layer 3 in LOPA frameworks; human-on-the-loop (HOTL) is viable for time-critical interventions.
Limitations: No empirical validation; all results illustrative only; does not address LLM-based agentic systems specifically; real-time computational constraints not experimentally quantified; common-cause failure analysis is conceptual only.
Future_Work: Systematic evaluation in digital twin environments; diverse fault scenarios and cyber-physical disturbances; domain-specific trade-off analysis before industrial deployment.
Research_Gap: No prior architecture formally separates performance AI from safety supervisory AI as independent LOPA-style protection layers for LLM-based agentic systems; runtime safety supervision for AI agents (vs. process control systems) remains unaddressed.
PreFlight_Relevance: Provides architectural precedent for PreFlight's independent scanning layer operating alongside deployed MCP agents; LOPA layer analogy maps to PreFlight's position as a pre-deployment safety gate; AI-ESH concept informs PreFlight's severity-based blocking recommendations.
